epigraph.text:
He determines the number of the stars; he gives to all of 
them their names.

content.text:
Integration by parts gave us a powerful way to handle products like
$x\,e^{x}$. But what about an integral like
\[
 \int \sin^{5}x\,\cos^{2}x\dx\,?
\]
This is a product of trigonometric functions raised to powers. No single
$u$-substitution immediately works, and integration by parts would be
painful. Instead, we will exploit the *Pythagorean identities* and
*half-angle formulas* to reduce these integrals to manageable forms.
The key insight is that the strategy depends on whether the powers are
**odd** or **even**.
Before diving into examples, let us collect the identities we will need.

The half-angle identities are particularly important: they convert squares
into first-degree expressions, making integration possible.
We treat three cases based on the parity of $n$ and $m$.

**Case 1: The power of sine is odd ($n$ odd).** Factor out one
$\sin x$ to pair with $dx$, and convert the remaining even powers of
$\sin x$ using $\sin^{2}x = 1 - \cos^{2}x$. Then let $u = \cos x$.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{3}x\dx$
content.solution:
Factor out one $\sin x$:
\[
 \int \sin^{3}x\dx = \int \sin^{2}x \cdot \sin x\dx
 = \int (1 - \cos^{2}x)\sin x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \cos x$, $du = -\sin x\dx$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= -\int (1 - u^{2})\,du = -\left(u - \frac{u^{3}}{3}\right) + C \\
 &= -\cos x + \frac{\cos^{3}x}{3} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{3}x\,\cos^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
The power of $\sin x$ is odd (3). Factor out one $\sin x$:
\[
 \int \sin^{3}x\,\cos^{4}x\dx
 = \int \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{4}x\,\sin x\dx.
\]
Replace $\sin^{2}x = 1 - \cos^{2}x$:
\[
 = \int (1 - \cos^{2}x)\,\cos^{4}x\,\sin x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \cos x$, $du = -\sin x\dx$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= -\int (1 - u^{2})\,u^{4}\,du
 = -\int (u^{4} - u^{6})\,du \\[4pt]
 &= -\frac{u^{5}}{5} + \frac{u^{7}}{7} + C
 = -\frac{\cos^{5}x}{5} + \frac{\cos^{7}x}{7} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{5}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\sin^{5}x = \sin^{4}x \cdot \sin x = (1 - \cos^{2}x)^{2}\sin x$.
Let $u = \cos x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sin^{5}x\dx
 &= -\int (1 - u^{2})^{2}\,du = -\int (1 - 2u^{2} + u^{4})\,du \\
 &= -u + \frac{2u^{3}}{3} - \frac{u^{5}}{5} + C \\
 &= -\cos x + \frac{2\cos^{3}x}{3} - \frac{\cos^{5}x}{5} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.text:
**Case 2: The power of cosine is odd ($m$ odd).** Factor out one
$\cos x$, convert the remaining even powers using $\cos^{2}x = 1 - \sin^{2}x$,
and substitute $u = \sin x$.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \cos^{5}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\cos^{5}x = \cos^{4}x \cdot \cos x = (1 - \sin^{2}x)^{2}\cos x$.
Let $u = \sin x$, $du = \cos x\dx$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \cos^{5}x\dx
 &= \int (1 - u^{2})^{2}\,du = \int (1 - 2u^{2} + u^{4})\,du \\
 &= u - \frac{2u^{3}}{3} + \frac{u^{5}}{5} + C \\
 &= \sin x - \frac{2\sin^{3}x}{3} + \frac{\sin^{5}x}{5} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{3}x\dx$
content.solution:
The power of $\cos x$ is odd (3). Factor out one $\cos x$:
\[
 \int \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{3}x\dx
 = \int \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{2}x\,\cos x\dx
 = \int \sin^{2}x\,(1-\sin^{2}x)\,\cos x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \sin x$, $du = \cos x\dx$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \int u^{2}(1 - u^{2})\,du
 = \int (u^{2} - u^{4})\,du \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{u^{3}}{3} - \frac{u^{5}}{5} + C
 = \frac{\sin^{3}x}{3} - \frac{\sin^{5}x}{5} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{4}x\,\cos^{5}x\dx$
content.solution:
The power of $\cos x$ is odd. Factor out one $\cos x$:
\[
 \int \sin^{4}x\,\cos^{5}x\dx = \int \sin^{4}x\,(1-\sin^{2}x)^{2}\cos x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \sin x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \int u^{4}(1 - u^{2})^{2}\,du = \int u^{4}(1 - 2u^{2} + u^{4})\,du \\
 &= \int (u^{4} - 2u^{6} + u^{8})\,du \\
 &= \frac{u^{5}}{5} - \frac{2u^{7}}{7} + \frac{u^{9}}{9} + C \\
 &= \frac{\sin^{5}x}{5} - \frac{2\sin^{7}x}{7} + \frac{\sin^{9}x}{9} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.text:
**Case 3: Both powers are even.** When both $m$ and $n$ are even,
no simple $u$-substitution works. Instead, we use the
**half-angle identities** to reduce powers.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{2}x\dx$
content.solution:
Apply the half-angle identity:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sin^{2}x\dx
 &= \int \frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}\dx
 = \frac{1}{2}\int (1 - \cos 2x)\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{1}{2}\left(x - \frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right) + C
 = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \cos^{2}x\dx$
content.solution:
Apply the half-angle identity:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \cos^{2}x\dx
 &= \int \frac{1 + \cos 2x}{2}\dx
 = \frac{1}{2}\int (1 + \cos 2x)\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{1}{2}\left(x + \frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right) + C
 = \frac{x}{2} + \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.text:
when the morning stars sang together and all the sons of
God shouted for joy?}

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\sin^{4}x = (\sin^{2}x)^{2}$ and apply the half-angle identity:
\[
 \sin^{4}x = \left(\frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}\right)^{2}
 = \frac{1 - 2\cos 2x + \cos^{2}2x}{4}.
\]
Now apply the half-angle identity to $\cos^{2}2x$:
\[
 \cos^{2}2x = \frac{1 + \cos 4x}{2}.
\]
Substituting:
\[
 \sin^{4}x = \frac{1}{4}\left(1 - 2\cos 2x + \frac{1 + \cos 4x}{2}\right)
 = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{3}{2} - 2\cos 2x + \frac{\cos 4x}{2}\right).
\]
Integrating:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sin^{4}x\dx
 &= \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{3x}{2} - \sin 2x + \frac{\sin 4x}{8}\right) + C \\
 &= \frac{3x}{8} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + \frac{\sin 4x}{32} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \cos^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\cos^{4}x = (\cos^{2}x)^{2} = \left(\frac{1 + \cos 2x}{2}\right)^{2}
= \frac{1 + 2\cos 2x + \cos^{2}2x}{4}$.

Using $\cos^{2}2x = \frac{1 + \cos 4x}{2}$:
\[
 \cos^{4}x = \frac{1}{4}\left(1 + 2\cos 2x + \frac{1 + \cos 4x}{2}\right)
 = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{3}{2} + 2\cos 2x + \frac{\cos 4x}{2}\right).
\]
Integrating:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \cos^{4}x\dx
 &= \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{3x}{2} + \sin 2x + \frac{\sin 4x}{8}\right) + C \\
 &= \frac{3x}{8} + \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + \frac{\sin 4x}{32} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{2}x\dx$
content.solution:
We may use the double-angle identity $\sin x\cos x = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2x$:
\[
 \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{2}x = (\sin x\cos x)^{2}
 = \left(\frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right)^{\!2}
 = \frac{\sin^{2}2x}{4}.
\]
Now apply the half-angle identity to $\sin^{2}2x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{\sin^{2}2x}{4}\dx
 &= \frac{1}{4}\int \frac{1 - \cos 4x}{2}\dx
 = \frac{1}{8}\int (1 - \cos 4x)\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{1}{8}\left(x - \frac{\sin 4x}{4}\right) + C
 = \frac{x}{8} - \frac{\sin 4x}{32} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Both powers are even. Write:
\[
 \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{4}x = \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{2}x\,\cos^{2}x
 = \frac{\sin^{2}2x}{4} \cdot \cos^{2}x.
\]
Alternatively, convert everything using half-angle formulas:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin^{2}x\,\cos^{4}x
 &= \frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{1 + \cos 2x}{2}\right)^{2} \\
 &= \frac{(1 - \cos 2x)(1 + \cos 2x)^{2}}{8}.
\end{aligned}$$
Let $c = \cos 2x$ for brevity. Then:
\[
 (1 - c)(1 + c)^{2} = (1 - c)(1 + 2c + c^{2}) = 1 + 2c + c^{2} - c - 2c^{2} - c^{3}
 = 1 + c - c^{2} - c^{3}.
\]
So:
\[
 \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{4}x = \frac{1 + \cos 2x - \cos^{2}2x - \cos^{3}2x}{8}.
\]
For $\cos^{2}2x$, use $\cos^{2}2x = \frac{1 + \cos 4x}{2}$.
For $\cos^{3}2x$, use $\cos^{3}2x = \cos 2x \cdot \frac{1 + \cos 4x}{2}
= \frac{\cos 2x + \cos 2x\cos 4x}{2}$.

Using the product-to-sum formula:
$\cos 2x\cos 4x = \frac{1}{2}(\cos 6x + \cos 2x)$.

After careful algebra:
\[
 \int \sin^{2}x\,\cos^{4}x\dx = \frac{x}{16} - \frac{\sin 4x}{64}
 + \frac{\sin^{3}2x}{48} + C.
\]
(Or equivalently: $\frac{x}{16} - \frac{\sin 4x}{64} - \frac{\sin 6x}{192}
+ \frac{\sin 2x}{64} + C$.)

content.text:
The pair $\tan x$ and $\sec x$ are linked by the identity
$\sec^{2}x = 1 + \tan^{2}x$, just as $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ are linked
by $\sin^{2}x + \cos^{2}x = 1$. We also have the key derivative facts:
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\tan x = \sec^{2}x,
 \qquad
 \deriv{}{x}\sec x = \sec x\,\tan x.
\]

content.title: Powers of Tangent and Secant
content.content:
**For $\displaystyle\int \tan^{n}x\,\sec^{m}x\dx$:**

1. If $m$ is even: factor out $\sec^{2}x$, convert remaining
 $\sec^{2}x$ factors using $\sec^{2}x = 1 + \tan^{2}x$, let $u = \tan x$.

2. If $n$ is odd: factor out $\sec x\,\tan x$, convert remaining
 $\tan^{2}x$ factors using $\tan^{2}x = \sec^{2}x - 1$, let $u = \sec x$.

3. Otherwise: reduce using $\tan^{2}x = \sec^{2}x - 1$ or
 integration by parts.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \tan^{2}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\tan^{2}x = \sec^{2}x - 1$:
\[
 \int \tan^{2}x\dx = \int (\sec^{2}x - 1)\dx
 = \tan x - x + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \tan^{3}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\tan^{3}x = \tan x \cdot \tan^{2}x = \tan x(\sec^{2}x - 1)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \tan^{3}x\dx
 &= \int \tan x\,\sec^{2}x\dx - \int \tan x\dx \\
 &= \frac{\tan^{2}x}{2} - \ln|\sec x| + C
 = \frac{\tan^{2}x}{2} + \ln|\cos x| + C.
\end{aligned}$$
For the first integral, we used $u = \tan x$, $du = \sec^{2}x\dx$.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \tan^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\tan^{4}x = \tan^{2}x \cdot \tan^{2}x = \tan^{2}x(\sec^{2}x - 1)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \tan^{4}x\dx
 &= \int \tan^{2}x\,\sec^{2}x\dx - \int \tan^{2}x\dx \\
 &= \frac{\tan^{3}x}{3} - (\tan x - x) + C \\
 &= \frac{\tan^{3}x}{3} - \tan x + x + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sec^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
The power of $\sec x$ is even. Factor out $\sec^{2}x$:
\[
 \int \sec^{4}x\dx = \int \sec^{2}x \cdot \sec^{2}x\dx
 = \int (1 + \tan^{2}x)\sec^{2}x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \tan x$, $du = \sec^{2}x\dx$:
\[
 = \int (1 + u^{2})\,du = u + \frac{u^{3}}{3} + C
 = \tan x + \frac{\tan^{3}x}{3} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \tan^{5}x\,\sec^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Since the power of $\sec x$ is even, factor out $\sec^{2}x$ and convert
the rest using $\sec^{2}x = 1 + \tan^{2}x$:
\[
 \int \tan^{5}x\,\sec^{4}x\dx
 = \int \tan^{5}x\,\sec^{2}x\,\sec^{2}x\dx
 = \int \tan^{5}x\,(1+\tan^{2}x)\,\sec^{2}x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \tan x$, $du = \sec^{2}x\dx$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \int u^{5}(1 + u^{2})\,du
 = \int (u^{5} + u^{7})\,du \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{u^{6}}{6} + \frac{u^{8}}{8} + C
 = \frac{\tan^{6}x}{6} + \frac{\tan^{8}x}{8} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \tan^{2}x\,\sec^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Factor out $\sec^{2}x$:
\[
 \int \tan^{2}x\,\sec^{4}x\dx = \int \tan^{2}x(1 + \tan^{2}x)\sec^{2}x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \tan x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \int u^{2}(1 + u^{2})\,du = \int (u^{2} + u^{4})\,du \\
 &= \frac{u^{3}}{3} + \frac{u^{5}}{5} + C
 = \frac{\tan^{3}x}{3} + \frac{\tan^{5}x}{5} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \tan^{3}x\,\sec^{3}x\dx$
content.solution:
When the power of $\tan x$ is odd, factor out $\sec x\,\tan x$ (for the
substitution $u = \sec x$, $du = \sec x\,\tan x\dx$). Rewrite:
\[
 \int \tan^{3}x\,\sec^{3}x\dx
 = \int \tan^{2}x\,\sec^{2}x\,(\sec x\,\tan x)\dx.
\]
Replace $\tan^{2}x = \sec^{2}x - 1$:
\[
 = \int (\sec^{2}x - 1)\,\sec^{2}x\,(\sec x\,\tan x)\dx.
\]
Let $u = \sec x$, $du = \sec x\,\tan x\dx$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \int (u^{2} - 1)\,u^{2}\,du
 = \int (u^{4} - u^{2})\,du \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{u^{5}}{5} - \frac{u^{3}}{3} + C
 = \frac{\sec^{5}x}{5} - \frac{\sec^{3}x}{3} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \tan^{3}x\,\sec x\dx$
content.solution:
Factor out $\sec x\tan x$:
\[
 \int \tan^{3}x\,\sec x\dx = \int \tan^{2}x \cdot \sec x\,\tan x\dx
 = \int (\sec^{2}x - 1)\sec x\,\tan x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \sec x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= \int (u^{2} - 1)\,du = \frac{u^{3}}{3} - u + C
 = \frac{\sec^{3}x}{3} - \sec x + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sec^{3}x\dx$
content.solution:
This important integral requires integration by parts. Let
$u = \sec x$, $dv = \sec^{2}x\dx$, so $du = \sec x\,\tan x\dx$,
$v = \tan x$. Then
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sec^{3}x\dx
 &= \sec x\,\tan x - \int \tan x \cdot \sec x\,\tan x\dx \\
 &= \sec x\,\tan x - \int \sec x\,\tan^{2}x\dx \\
 &= \sec x\,\tan x - \int \sec x\,(\sec^{2}x - 1)\dx \\
 &= \sec x\,\tan x - \int \sec^{3}x\dx + \int \sec x\dx.
\end{aligned}$$
Add $\displaystyle\int \sec^{3}x\dx$ to both sides:
\[
 2\int \sec^{3}x\dx = \sec x\,\tan x + \ln\abs{\sec x + \tan x}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
 \int \sec^{3}x\dx
 = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(\sec x\,\tan x
 + \ln\abs{\sec x + \tan x}\bigr) + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sec^{5}x\dx$
content.solution:
Use integration by parts with $u = \sec^{3}x$ and $dv = \sec^{2}x\dx$.
Then $du = 3\sec^{3}x\tan x\dx$ and $v = \tan x$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sec^{5}x\dx
 &= \sec^{3}x\tan x - 3\int \sec^{3}x\tan^{2}x\dx \\
 &= \sec^{3}x\tan x - 3\int \sec^{3}x(\sec^{2}x - 1)\dx \\
 &= \sec^{3}x\tan x - 3\int \sec^{5}x\dx + 3\int \sec^{3}x\dx.
\end{aligned}$$
Solving for $\int\sec^{5}x\dx$:
\[
 4\int \sec^{5}x\dx = \sec^{3}x\tan x + 3\int \sec^{3}x\dx.
\]
Using the result from Example [ex:trig-sec3]:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sec^{5}x\dx
 &= \frac{1}{4}\sec^{3}x\tan x
 + \frac{3}{8}\bigl(\sec x\tan x + \ln|\sec x + \tan x|\bigr) + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.text:
The techniques for $\cot x$ and $\csc x$ parallel those for $\tan x$ and
$\sec x$. The key identity is $\csc^{2}x = 1 + \cot^{2}x$, and the
derivatives are:
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\cot x = -\csc^{2}x,
 \qquad
 \deriv{}{x}\csc x = -\csc x\,\cot x.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \cot^{2}x\dx$
content.solution:
Write $\cot^{2}x = \csc^{2}x - 1$:
\[
 \int \cot^{2}x\dx = \int (\csc^{2}x - 1)\dx = -\cot x - x + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \csc^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Factor out $\csc^{2}x$:
\[
 \int \csc^{4}x\dx = \int (1 + \cot^{2}x)\csc^{2}x\dx.
\]
Let $u = \cot x$, $du = -\csc^{2}x\dx$:
$$\begin{aligned}
&= -\int (1 + u^{2})\,du = -u - \frac{u^{3}}{3} + C
 = -\cot x - \frac{\cot^{3}x}{3} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.text: Products of Sines and Cosines with Different Arguments

How do we evaluate integrals like $\int \sin(3x)\cos(5x)\dx$? The
**product-to-sum formulas** convert such products into sums.

content.title: Product-to-Sum Formulas
content.content:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin A\,\cos B &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)\bigr], \\
 \sin A\,\sin B &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)\bigr], \\
 \cos A\,\cos B &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\bigr].
\end{aligned}$$

content.text:
These formulas are derived from the angle-addition formulas and are
essential in Fourier analysis, signal processing, and acoustics.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin(3x)\cos(5x)\dx$
content.solution:
Apply the first product-to-sum formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sin(3x)\cos(5x)\dx
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\sin(8x) + \sin(-2x)\bigr]\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\sin(8x) - \sin(2x)\bigr]\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{\cos 8x}{8} + \frac{\cos 2x}{2}\right) + C \\[4pt]
 &= -\frac{\cos 8x}{16} + \frac{\cos 2x}{4} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin(4x)\sin(6x)\dx$
content.solution:
Using $\sin A\sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \sin(4x)\sin(6x)\dx
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\cos(4x - 6x) - \cos(4x + 6x)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\cos(-2x) - \cos(10x)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\cos(2x) - \cos(10x)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sin 2x}{2} - \frac{\sin 10x}{10}\right) + C \\
 &= \frac{\sin 2x}{4} - \frac{\sin 10x}{20} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \cos(2x)\cos(5x)\dx$
content.solution:
Using $\cos A\cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int \cos(2x)\cos(5x)\dx
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\cos(2x - 5x) + \cos(2x + 5x)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\cos(-3x) + \cos(7x)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int\bigl[\cos(3x) + \cos(7x)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sin 3x}{3} + \frac{\sin 7x}{7}\right) + C \\
 &= \frac{\sin 3x}{6} + \frac{\sin 7x}{14} + C.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \sin x\sin(2x)\sin(3x)\dx$
content.solution:
First combine $\sin x\sin(2x)$:
\[
 \sin x\sin(2x) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(x - 2x) - \cos(x + 2x)]
 = \frac{1}{2}[\cos x - \cos 3x].
\]
So:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin x\sin(2x)\sin(3x)
 &= \frac{1}{2}[\cos x - \cos 3x]\sin 3x \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\cos x\sin 3x - \frac{1}{2}\cos 3x\sin 3x.
\end{aligned}$$
For $\cos x\sin 3x$: $\cos x\sin 3x = \frac{1}{2}[\sin 4x + \sin 2x]$.

For $\cos 3x\sin 3x = \frac{1}{2}\sin 6x$.

Thus:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin x\sin(2x)\sin(3x)
 &= \frac{1}{4}[\sin 4x + \sin 2x] - \frac{1}{4}\sin 6x \\
 &= \frac{1}{4}[\sin 2x + \sin 4x - \sin 6x].
\end{aligned}$$
Integrating:
\[
 \int \sin x\sin(2x)\sin(3x)\dx
 = \frac{1}{4}\left(-\frac{\cos 2x}{2} - \frac{\cos 4x}{4}
 + \frac{\cos 6x}{6}\right) + C.
\]

content.text:
The definite integrals of trigonometric products over certain intervals
reveal remarkable *orthogonality* properties that underlie Fourier
analysis.

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}^{\pi/2}\sin^{3}x\cos^{2}x\dx$
content.solution:
Using $\sin^{3}x = (1 - \cos^{2}x)\sin x$ and $u = \cos x$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\defint{0}^{\pi/2}\sin^{3}x\cos^{2}x\dx
 &= -\defint{1}{0}(1 - u^{2})u^{2}\,du = \defint{0}{1}(u^{2} - u^{4})\,du \\
 &= \left[\frac{u^{3}}{3} - \frac{u^{5}}{5}\right]_{0}^{1}
 = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{5} = \frac{2}{15}.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}^{\pi}\sin^{2}x\dx$
content.solution:
Using the half-angle formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
\defint{0}^{\pi}\sin^{2}x\dx
 &= \defint{0}^{\pi}\frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}\dx
 = \frac{1}{2}\left[x - \frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi} \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[(\pi - 0) - (0 - 0)\bigr] = \frac{\pi}{2}.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}^{\pi/2}\cos^{4}x\dx$
content.solution:
Using the result from Example [ex:trig-cos4]:
$$\begin{aligned}
\defint{0}^{\pi/2}\cos^{4}x\dx
 &= \left[\frac{3x}{8} + \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + \frac{\sin 4x}{32}
 \right]_{0}^{\pi/2} \\
 &= \frac{3\pi/2}{8} + 0 + 0 - 0 = \frac{3\pi}{16}.
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Trigonometric Integrals
content.content:
**For $\displaystyle\int \sin^{n}x\,\cos^{m}x\dx$:**

1. If $n$ is odd: factor out $\sin x$, use $\sin^{2}x = 1 - \cos^{2}x$,
 let $u = \cos x$.

2. If $m$ is odd: factor out $\cos x$, use $\cos^{2}x = 1 - \sin^{2}x$,
 let $u = \sin x$.

3. If both are even: use half-angle identities to reduce powers.

**For $\displaystyle\int \tan^{n}x\,\sec^{m}x\dx$:**

1. If $m$ is even: factor out $\sec^{2}x$, use
 $\sec^{2}x = 1 + \tan^{2}x$, let $u = \tan x$.

2. If $n$ is odd: factor out $\sec x\,\tan x$, use
 $\tan^{2}x = \sec^{2}x - 1$, let $u = \sec x$.

3. Otherwise: reduce using $\tan^{2}x = \sec^{2}x - 1$ or
 integration by parts (e.g., $\int \sec^{3}x\dx$).

**For $\displaystyle\int \sin(mx)\cos(nx)\dx$** (different arguments):
use product-to-sum formulas.

content.title: Joseph Fourier (1768--1830)
content.content:
Joseph Fourier discovered that *any* periodic function can be
represented as a sum of sines and cosines—the celebrated *Fourier
series*. The integrals in this section, particularly the product-to-sum
formulas and integrals of $\sin(mx)\cos(nx)$, lie at the heart of Fourier
analysis. Fourier was deeply motivated by physical problems: his work on
heat conduction transformed both mathematics and physics. He accompanied
Napoleon to Egypt and later became a Baron and secretary of the French
Academy of Sciences.

content.title: The Ancient Roots of Trigonometry
content.content:
Trigonometry has ancient origins in Babylonian and Greek astronomy.
Hipparchus of Nicaea (c. 190–120 BCE) compiled the first trigonometric
tables to predict celestial phenomena. The word "sine" comes from a
fascinating linguistic journey: the Sanskrit *jya* (bowstring) was
transliterated into Arabic as *jiba*, which was then mistaken for
the Arabic word *jayb* (bay or fold) and translated into Latin as
*sinus*. Thus our mathematical terminology preserves an ancient
error!

marginNotes.text:
For his invisible attributes, namely, his
eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the
creation of the world, in the things that have been made.

The heavens declare the glory of God, and the
sky above proclaims his handiwork.

Where were you when I laid the foundation of
the earth? …{

If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God, who
gives generously to all without reproach, and it will be given him.

For the Lord gives wisdom; from his mouth
come knowledge and understanding.

I turned my heart to know and to search
out and to seek wisdom and the scheme of things.

If even lifeless instruments, such as
the flute or the harp, do not give distinct notes, how will anyone know what
is played?

Lift up your eyes on high and see: who created
these? He who brings out their host by number, calling them all by name.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Nothing takes place in the world whose meaning
is not that of some maximum or minimum.

marginNotes.author: Pierre-Simon Laplace
marginNotes.text:
Read Euler, read Euler, he is the master
of us all.

marginNotes.author: Joseph Fourier
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics compares the most diverse phenomena
and discovers the secret analogies that unite them.
